method3.generated_article:
==Geography==
Kavachi is a shallow submarine stratovolcano in the southwestern Pacific Ocean, located in the western Solomon Islands about 24 km south of Vangunu Island.[2] It lies within the Pacific Ring of Fire at a convergent plate boundary, where subduction drives frequent volcanic activity.[1][2]

The volcano rises more than 1,200 m from the seafloor, which is around 1,100 m deep in the area of the main edifice.[2][4] Kavachi is roughly conical in shape and about 8 km in diameter at its base.[4] Its summit is very close to the sea surface, with reports placing it about 20 m below sea level, while the crater rim averages about 24 m depth.[2][5]

Kavachi’s summit crater is oblong and pockmarked, measuring about 120 m by 75 m, with the deepest soundings on the main peak reaching about 70 m water depth.[5] The volcano’s flanks descend at about 18° into surrounding depths greater than 1,000 m.[5] A secondary summit, described as a southwest extension, lies about 1.3 km southwest of the main summit and rises to about 260 m below sea level.[5]

Because the summit is so shallow, eruptions can discolor surrounding seawater and sometimes build temporary islands above the surface.[2][3] Kavachi has produced ephemeral islands at least twice in the past century, and one eruptive cycle in 2003 briefly formed an island about 15 m high before the summit subsided again below sea level.[4] This combination of shallow depth, active crater morphology, and repeated island-building makes Kavachi one of the most notable submarine volcanoes in the Pacific.[2][3]

==Eruptions==
Kavachi is among the most active submarine volcanoes in the southwest Pacific, with historically documented eruptions beginning in 1939 and recurring at frequent intervals thereafter.[1] Since that first recorded eruption, temporary islands have formed at least nine times, but these short-lived edifices were repeatedly removed by wave erosion within months.[1]

Activity in late 1951 was associated with frequent small tsunamis generated by the volcano.[1] Reported eruption histories compiled for Kavachi indicate persistent activity through the second half of the 20th century and into the 21st century, with the Smithsonian Global Volcanism Program listing 33 historical eruptions and noting that the volcano has been active during roughly half of the years since 1939.[1]

In May 2000, scientists aboard the CSIRO research vessel Franklin observed a renewed island-building phase after about nine years of dormancy.[2] On 14 May, eruptions threw ash and incandescent lava blocks up to 70 m above sea level, while sulfur-rich steam plumes rose to about 500 m.[2] Contemporary reports described the summit area as building a sandy, ashy beach just below sea level.[2]

Another eruptive episode in late 2001 and early 2002 produced daily explosive eruptions between 27 November and 13 December 2001.[2] On 13 January 2002, observers reported volcanic debris, gas bubbles, and frequent loud noises, with activity more vigorous than that seen in December 2001.[2]

During the 2003 eruptive cycle, a small island about 15 m high briefly formed, but by 16 November the summit had subsided to about 32 m below sea level.[2] A report issued later that month stated that no evidence of volcanic activity had been observed for the first time in four years.[2] Activity was again observed in March 2004 after that interval of quiescence.[2]

A January 2015 expedition coincided with a rare lull in eruptive activity, allowing researchers to enter the active crater and survey the summit area directly.[3] The study noted that Kavachi’s normally explosive behavior had previously restricted most observations to surface imagery and water-column measurements away from the summit.[3] Measurements made during the lull documented ongoing hydrothermal discharge, including warm water and carbon dioxide venting from within the crater, indicating that the system remained active even in the absence of surface eruptions.[4]

==Marine life==
Kavachi has attracted scientific attention for reports of marine organisms inhabiting its crater during quiescent intervals despite chemically and thermally extreme conditions.[2][4] Observations from expeditions and robotic surveys have recorded sharks, reef fish, gelatinous zooplankton, and microbial communities in the crater waters.[4][5]

The volcano became especially notable after cameras documented large predatory sharks inside the crater, contributing to its popular nickname “Sharkcano” or “Sharkano”.[1][2][5] Species reported from the crater include the scalloped hammerhead (*Sphyrna lewini*) and the silky shark (*Carcharhinus falciformis*).[4] These records were described as unexpected because the crater environment is affected by volcanic gases, elevated temperatures, acidity, and low-oxygen conditions.[4]

Measurements reported from the crater indicate water temperatures substantially above the surrounding sea and acidic conditions associated with carbon dioxide- and sulfur-rich emissions.[1][4] One account cited temperatures up to about 42 °C and an estimated pH of about 6.1 over the crater.[4] Another source described crater waters as highly acidic, with pH values of about 1–2 during active hydrothermal conditions.[2]

Researchers have proposed several explanations for how sharks may persist in or use the crater, including entering during calmer periods, leaving before eruptive episodes, detecting chemical or seismic warning signals, or possessing unusual tolerance to heat and acidity, though these hypotheses remain uncertain.[1][5] Because of these conditions, Kavachi has been described as a natural laboratory for studying extremophile ecosystems, marine adaptation, and the biological effects of volcanic alteration of ocean chemistry.[1][5]

Microbial life has also been highlighted as an important component of the crater ecosystem.[4][5] Suspended particles within the hydrothermal plume may support chemosynthetic bacteria, including reported *Sulfurimonas*, which use sulfur compounds and carbon dioxide as energy sources.[4] The assemblage of sharks, fishes, zooplankton, and microbes at Kavachi has therefore been cited in discussions of marine resilience under environmental stress and changing ocean conditions.[1][4][5]
